metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit acceptance criterion (or extend AC3) verifying that `from rebar import RebarError` and `from rebar import ConcurrencyError` remain importable after the move — e.g. a unit test in `tests/unit/test_errors_backcompat.py` that imports both names from the facade.
findings.finding: The described back-compat guarantee — that `rebar/__init__.py` re-exports `RebarError` and `ConcurrencyError` after they move to `rebar._errors` — has no corresponding acceptance criterion that verifies the re-export is actually present and importable by external callers.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'and `from rebar import RebarError` / `from rebar import ConcurrencyError` remain importable (verified by a new `tests/unit/test_errors_backcompat.py`)'. Add a two-line test: `from rebar import RebarError, ConcurrencyError` asserted importable, so the re-export is a hard requirement not just a stated intent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's scope description for `pai_tools.py`: the `show_ticket` inner function (line 155) is a read-only `import rebar` site that should also be rerouted to `from rebar import _reads`, not left as a facade import.
findings.finding: The plan states `pai_tools.py` keeps `import rebar` 'only in scopes that also do writes (comment/tag/edit_ticket)'. This is incorrect: the `show_ticket` nested function (line 155) uses `import rebar` then calls only `rebar.show_ticket` — a pure read present in `_reads` — with no write in scope.
findings.location: Item 9.3 — concrete scope / reroute call-sites; `src/rebar/llm/pai_tools.py` lines 150–160
findings.suggested_fix: In `pai_tools.py` `show_ticket` inner function, replace `import rebar; rebar.show_ticket(...)` with `from rebar import _reads; _reads.show_ticket(...)`. The `comment_ticket` inner function (line 169) correctly keeps `import rebar` for the write.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Audit all `import rebar` scopes in `steps.py` and confirm each pure-read scope (`fetch_ticket`, `fetch_commits`, `fetch_epic_graph`, overlay_triggers read-path) is also rerouted to `from rebar import _reads`, or update the plan's scope description to explain why they are intentionally kept as facade calls.
findings.finding: The plan states `steps.py` keeps `import rebar` 'only in scopes that also do writes'. Code inspection shows at least four scopes — `fetch_ticket` (line 61), `fetch_commits` (line 87), `fetch_epic_graph` (line 103), and the structural-trigger read-path of `overlay_triggers` (line 151/164) — use only `rebar.show_ticket` or `rebar.deps`, both of which are in `_reads`. These are pure-read scopes with no write call.
findings.location: Item 9.3 — concrete scope / reroute call-sites; `src/rebar/llm/workflow/steps.py` lines 61, 87, 103, 151

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether `attest.py`'s `current_material_fingerprint` function (line 796) — which calls only `rebar.show_ticket` / `rebar.list_tickets` — should also be rerouted to `from rebar import _reads`, and update the plan's attest.py scope description accordingly.
findings.finding: `attest.py` line 796 (`current_material_fingerprint`) has an `import rebar` scope that calls only `rebar.show_ticket` and `rebar.list_tickets` — both in `_reads` — yet the plan's description implies attest.py only retains `import rebar` 'in scopes that also do... reads not in _reads (verify_signature/get_file_impact)'. This pure-reads scope is not accounted for.
findings.location: Item 9.3 — concrete scope / reroute call-sites; `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py` lines 796–805

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a dedicated unit test (e.g. `tests/unit/test_no_reads_facade_cycle.py`) that asserts `rebar._reads` does not import `rebar` (the facade), mirroring the `test_no_reducer_engine_support_cycle.py` pattern established in change 205.
findings.finding: The plan establishes a pattern (in part 2 / change 205) of adding a dedicated unit test asserting the cycle is broken (`test_no_reducer_engine_support_cycle.py`), but the AC for part 3 includes no equivalent test explicitly asserting that `rebar._reads` does not import the `rebar` facade — relying solely on the baseline ratchet and full-suite green.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — item 3: '`rebar._reads` no longer imports the `rebar` facade'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `tests/unit/test_no_reads_facade_cycle.py` with a test that does `import importlib; import rebar._reads; assert 'rebar' not in [m.__name__ for m in ...]` or uses grimp/importlib to assert the `rebar._reads → rebar` edge is absent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief note to CLAUDE.md (or an ADR) documenting the `rebar._errors` leaf-module pattern as the approved mechanism for breaking facade back-edges, so future contributors know to add new shared exceptions there rather than to `rebar/__init__.py`.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new architectural pattern (a stdlib-only leaf error module `rebar._errors` as the canonical mechanism for breaking SCC back-edges) but does not plan to capture this decision in an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design doc update — future contributors may unknowingly re-introduce facade imports for other error classes, recreating the cycle.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a section to CLAUDE.md such as: 'Error classes shared across internal layers MUST live in `rebar._errors` (stdlib-only, no internal imports) and be re-exported from `rebar/__init__.py` for back-compat. Do NOT import from the facade inside `_reads` or other leaf modules.' This makes the CI ratchet's intent legible.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `rebar._errors` has ≥3 internal (non-facade) consumers at ship time, OR document in the plan/commit message which additional internal modules will migrate to it in near-term follow-ons (so the new leaf is not a single-consumer abstraction on day 1).
findings.finding: `src/rebar/_errors.py` is a new leaf abstraction with only one confirmed direct internal consumer at ship time (`_reads.py`, line 45), which does not meet the Rule-of-Three threshold for justifying a new shared module.
findings.suggested_fix: If no other module will consume `_errors` directly in this or the next planned change, consider an inline alternative: move the `_rebar_error` factory in `_reads.py` to import from `rebar._commands._seam` or another already-leaf error source, or simply define a minimal private `_RebarError` stub inside `_reads.py` itself for the one callsite, avoiding a new shared file until a second consumer exists.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `from rebar import signing` (attest.py line 758, `claim_gate_check`) to the plan's list of retained facade edges in attest.py, so the per-scope rationale is complete.
findings.finding: `llm/plan_review/attest.py` `claim_gate_check` (line 757) contains `from rebar import signing` (line 758) — a facade import that is NOT in `_reads` and is not mentioned anywhere in the reroute plan. The plan says attest.py retains `import rebar` only for `verify_signature`/`get_file_impact` scopes but does not acknowledge this additional `from rebar import signing` edge as a consumer that stays.
findings.location: Item 9.3 — concrete scope / Reroute read call-sites bullet; `llm/plan_review/attest.py` line 757-758
findings.suggested_fix: Amend the attest.py entry to: 'keeps `import rebar` / `from rebar import signing` in scopes using `get_file_impact`, `verify_signature`, or `signing.*` directly.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for AC4 — e.g. a `grep -rn 'import rebar' llm/spec_scan.py llm/operations.py ... | grep -v '_reads'` assertion (or a dedicated unit test analogous to `test_no_reducer_engine_support_cycle.py`) that fails if any enumerated file still contains a bare `import rebar` in a read-only scope.
findings.finding: AC4 ('Read-only function-local import rebar sites use from rebar import _reads') has no concrete proving command that verifies the structural change actually occurred in the enumerated files; 'suite green' only proves behavioral equivalence, not that the imports were rerouted.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — fourth bullet ('Read-only function-local import rebar sites…')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a unit test `tests/unit/test_no_llm_facade_import.py` that uses `ast.parse` + `importlib` to assert that the enumerated modules do not contain module-level or function-local `import rebar` (only `from rebar import _reads` or similar), mirroring the pattern already established by `test_no_reducer_engine_support_cycle.py`. Reference this test in AC4 as its proving command.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly enumerate which functions in `llm/workflow/steps.py` are rerouted to `from rebar import _reads` (fetch_ticket, fetch_commits, fetch_epic_graph, overlay_triggers pure-read sites) versus which retain `import rebar` (comment_verdict, tag_step, set_fields write-mixed sites).
findings.finding: `llm/workflow/steps.py` has four pure-read function-local `import rebar` sites (lines 61, 87, 103, 151 — `fetch_ticket`, `fetch_commits`, `fetch_epic_graph`, `overlay_triggers` using only `show_ticket`/`deps`) that should be rerouted to `from rebar import _reads`, but the plan only groups steps.py in the 'others keep it only in write scopes' bucket without enumerating which specific functions get rerouted vs retained. An implementor reading the plan cannot tell whether these four read-only sites are rerouted or left as `import rebar`.
findings.location: Item 9.3 — concrete scope / Reroute read call-sites bullet; `llm/workflow/steps.py` lines 61, 87, 103, 151
findings.suggested_fix: Add a per-function breakdown for steps.py analogous to the existing per-file breakdown: 'fetch_ticket / fetch_commits / fetch_epic_graph / overlay_triggers switch to `from rebar import _reads`; comment_verdict / tag_step / set_fields retain `import rebar` (write path).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether `llm/eval_solver.py`'s module-scope `import rebar` (line 21) contributes to the `llm::rebar` SCC edge; if so, add it to the reroute list or document why it is intentionally excluded.
findings.finding: `eval_solver.py` has `import rebar` at MODULE SCOPE (line 21), not function-local, yet it is absent from the plan's enumerated reroute list. This module-level import keeps `llm::rebar` in the SCC regardless of the reroutes the plan executes, and it calls only `rebar.show_ticket`/`rebar.list_tickets` (both in `_reads`).
findings.location: Item 9.3 — concrete scope / reroute call-sites list; `src/rebar/llm/eval_solver.py` line 21
findings.suggested_fix: In `eval_solver.py`, make `import rebar` function-local (inside the functions that call `rebar.*`) or switch the pure-read calls (`rebar.show_ticket`/`rebar.list_tickets`) to `from rebar import _reads` to match the reroute pattern.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
